title: Growing Up in Hawaii
summary: Cecilia Suyat Marshall recalls having no awareness of American racial hierarchy growing up in Hawaii, where she attended school alongside Japanese, Filipino, and Chinese classmates and knew of only one Negro family in her section. She says it was not until she went to New York that she found out about the racial problem.

title: Arriving in New York
summary: Marshall describes loving New York after arriving, enrolling at Columbia University for a stenographic session, and being told she must support herself if she wanted to stay. She recounts going to the employment office, which sent her to the NAACP National Office in New York.

title: Learning the Branch Department Job
summary: Marshall recounts working for the director of branches of the National Office, where she learned about the racial problems for the first time. Through the job she met local people, attended annual conventions in different cities, and was always there to take the minutes.

title: Meeting Walter White and Roy Wilkins
summary: Marshall describes meeting executive secretary Walter White, whom she called Pops, and Roy Wilkins, and how White eventually gave her away in marriage to Thurgood Marshall, the legal counsel. She confirms she worked as secretary to Gloster B. Current, director of branches.

title: Walter White's Marriage Controversy
summary: Marshall describes her day-to-day correspondence work and meeting people who came to the city for help. She recounts how Walter White divorcing his Black wife and marrying a white woman practically broke up the whole organization, which shaped her fear when Thurgood later proposed.

title: Refusing and Accepting the Proposal
summary: Marshall recalls telling Thurgood no when he proposed, fearing she would be seen as a foreigner and split the organization. She recounts his insistence that he was marrying her and not the country, and how that is how they got married.

title: The Local People Were the Heroes
summary: Marshall says she felt blessed to be at the organization and got to know local people in the South who took the lawyers into their homes when hotels would not. She relays Thurgood's view that the lawyers were cowards who argued a case and left, while local people who put their names on briefs stayed to face hostility and were the real heroes.

title: Hosts in Atlanta and Chattanooga
summary: Marshall recalls she cannot remember all the names of the people who hosted her in Atlanta, Chattanooga, and Oklahoma City, but says they treated her as a daughter. She mentions having recently reviewed NAACP papers at the Library of Congress about the annual conventions.

title: Groveland Four and Emmett Till
summary: Marshall recalls being present for the cases of the Groveland Four, indicted over allegations involving a white woman, and remembers the Emmett Till case. She explains that branches were responsible for handling cases locally and got advice from the office's lawyers and from her boss, who headed all the branches in the country.

title: Hearing Strategy and Seeing Change
summary: Marshall says taking notes let her hear a great deal of strategy and learn about her country and what was being done. Looking back, she reflects that the movement has come a long way but still has a way to go, citing being able to enter restaurants and hotels without being turned away.

title: The Bible and the Gun
summary: Marshall recounts being fed by local people at conventions when restaurants would not serve them, then riding in a car with Thurgood when a branch member opened the glove compartment to show a Bible and a gun. The member said they would use the Bible first, and if that did not work, the gun, illustrating how people lived in those times.

title: Helping on Brown v. Board
summary: Marshall explains that she worked in the branch department and not in the legal work, but when the team ran short of help on Brown v. Board of Education they needed stenographic help and she stepped in, since they were in the same office. She recalls reading about a meeting in Atlanta after the decision and the anticipation that change would come quickly.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954)

title: Our Work Has Just Begun
summary: Marshall recounts that after celebrating the victory, Thurgood said their work had just begun, marking him as a realist about the resistance that followed. She places the moment at the office about an hour into the celebration following Brown v. Board of Education or one of the big cases.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954)

title: Known to the Branches as Family
summary: Marshall explains that she did not want to break up the organization by marrying, but the people received her warmly because they had seen her around the branches and conventions recording the minutes. She describes being the first person local people reached in the branch department and helping them without bothering the director.

title: A Lesson From Hawaii
summary: Marshall reflects that her experience was a lesson and a blessing, contrasting growing up in Hawaii among a mix of people and languages with the divisions she encountered on the mainland. She recalls beginning to understand racial differences only after coming to New York.

title: Testing the Segregated Dining Car
summary: Marshall recalls that about a week after the Henderson v. United States dining-car ruling she boarded a train to Chattanooga with attorney Franklin H. Williams, who she notes later became a Negro ambassador to Ghana. Her boss, whom colleagues called Bubo, and the membership secretary both declined to go to the dining car, so she went with Williams.

title: Seated in the Center of the Car
summary: Marshall describes the head waiter unable to classify either Williams or herself, with her long hair and a flower, and seating them next to the partitioned section. Williams insisted in French until they were moved to the center of the dining car, and Marshall says she never felt nervous because she was new and did not know the rules.

title: Stopping Work and the Sit-Ins
summary: Marshall says she stopped working at the NAACP in 1955 but kept in close contact through Thurgood's legal work. She recalls segregation vividly and wishes Thurgood and his lawyers were present to see later progress, naming Robert L. Carter as one of his first assistants and Mildred Bond Roxborough as a secretary under her boss.

title: Lingering Differences and Shelby County
summary: Marshall observes that she still gets nasty looks at times and can feel the difference even when served. She comments on Shelby County v. Holder, noting that while much progress has been made, the court has gone back and invalidated some protections, but that progress continues.

title: Moving to Washington and Memorable Cases
summary: Marshall describes the move from New York City to Washington, D.C., saying she had gotten used to moving. Pressed on memorable cases, she names Brown v. Board of Education as the most important and notes that at her age she does not want to pull out and rehash the cases of the 1950s.

title: Family in Hawaii and a First Phone Call
summary: Marshall says her siblings did not follow her to work in New York and most are now deceased except one sister, and that she rarely returns to Hawaii because of the long trip. She recalls her first long-distance phone call home around 1948 or 1949, which she could barely afford on thirty-five dollars a week, and how she and her mother cried.

title: Inside the National Office
summary: Marshall describes the layout of the NAACP National Office: a stenographic pool where she began, a branch department overseeing roughly 1500 branches, a legal department supporting branch litigation, and Roy Wilkins and Walter White upstairs. She recalls the office felt like a family that would sit together and go to dinner after work.

title: Portraits of the Leadership
summary: Marshall sketches the people she worked with: Walter White as a wonderful administrator who was sometimes above everyone, Roy Wilkins as the one who did the work and met people locally, and Thurgood as her boss. She says it was like a family, especially at conferences and conventions.

title: Conventions Across the Country
summary: Marshall lists the convention cities she traveled to, beginning with her first in Boston in 1950, then Atlanta, Oklahoma City, Chicago, and Dallas, Texas. She explains that she got to know delegates because they came to her boss with branch problems while she was there.

title: Credit to the People
summary: Marshall reflects that the delegates deserve real credit for what they did in those times and that they treated the national staff like kings and queens though they did not have to. She frames herself as someone they could call on and work with while they struggled where they were.

title: Advice for Young People
summary: Marshall says young people should not take anything for granted, because people in earlier days went through sacrifices and indignities to build what exists today. She remarks that younger generations take much for granted and praises the older generations in the South as wonderful people.

title: Faith in God as Foundation
summary: Marshall notes she traveled in the South until 1955, then turns to faith, saying her deep faith in God was what carried them through. She describes talking to God in private to get everything out of her system and credits the church as sustaining structure.

title: Edgecombe Avenue to Morningside Gardens
summary: Marshall recalls first living at 409 Edgecombe Avenue, considered the high society of the Negro world, before moving into Morningside Gardens, the first middle-income cooperative in New York. She describes it as wonderful, with friends coming to them because the family had two young boys.

title: Neighbors in the Community
summary: Marshall describes the Morningside Gardens community and its neighbors, including Edward R. Dudley, who became ambassador to Liberia, attorney Robert L. Carter, Richard Parrish of the urban league, dentist George Dockery, and artist Louis Jefferson. She recalls the Dockery and Parrish wives competing to leave food at the door for her boys.

title: Moves Within Washington
summary: Marshall recounts the family's Washington residences, beginning at 64 G Street within walking distance of the church, then a three-story cooperative whose steps proved too much for Thurgood. After a year they looked toward Virginia.

title: Integrating Lake Barcroft
summary: Marshall describes moving to Lake Barcroft, Virginia after Attorney General Ramsey Clark told Thurgood the family had to come and integrate the place. She reports the family remained, as far as she knew, the only Black household there and that neighborhood kids welcomed her boys when they first moved in.

title: Board Service and Organizations
summary: Marshall lists organizations she was involved in, including the National Capitol Auxiliary of Supreme Court wives drawn from the district, Maryland, and Virginia, Travelers Aid, and the Legal Defense Fund board. She explains that on the boards they decided on policies and which cases to take, and again names Brown v. Board of Education as the case that stands out.
related_events: NAACP Legal Defense Fund board service

title: An Ordinary Person, Blessed
summary: Marshall describes herself as just an ordinary person doing ordinary things, blessed to have gone through what she did coming from Hawaii. Responding to a quote about ordinary people doing extraordinary things, she repeats her modest self-assessment and says she feels blessed and did what she had to do, staying home with the children while Thurgood was away.

title: A Charmed Life and Her Son
summary: Marshall closes by saying it has been a blessing and that she has lived a charmed life, joking about her sons' ages. She speaks of her son John W. Marshall, who began at the Marshals and became director of the United States Marshals Service and was then with the Transportation Department, as the interview ends.
